content:
# Genesis Chapter 17 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 9v-10r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_017_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_017_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 12-13 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0016

---

*El Shaddai Appears to Abram (vv. 1–2)*

**17:1** And Abram [וְאַבְרָם] was the son of ninety years and nine years [בֶּן-תִּשְׁעִים שָׁנָה וְתֵשַׁע שָׁנִים] and the LORD appeared [וַיֵּרָא יְהוָה] to Abram [אֶל-אַבְרָם] and said to him [וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו]: I am El Shaddai [אֲנִי-אֵל שַׁדַּי] — walk [הִתְהַלֵּךְ] before me [לְפָנַי] and be whole [וֶהְיֵה תָמִים].

**17:2** And I will place [וְאֶתְּנָה] my covenant [בְּרִיתִי] between me and between you [בֵּינִי וּבֵינֶךָ] and I will multiply you [וְאַרְבֶּה אוֹתְךָ] exceedingly [בִּמְאֹד מְאֹד].

---

*Abram Falls on His Face — The Promise Expanded (vv. 3–8)*

**17:3** And Abram fell [וַיִּפֹּל אַבְרָם] on his face [עַל-פָּנָיו] and God spoke [וַיְדַבֵּר אִתּוֹ אֱלֹהִים] with him saying [לֵאמֹר]:

**17:4** As for me [אֲנִי] — look [הִנֵּה] — my covenant is with you [בְּרִיתִי אִתָּךְ] and you will be [וְהָיִיתָ] for a father of a multitude of nations [לְאַב הֲמוֹן גּוֹיִם].

**17:5** And your name [וְלֹא-יִקָּרֵא שִׁמְךָ] will no longer be called [עוֹד] Abram [אַבְרָם] and your name will be [וְהָיָה שִׁמְךָ] Abraham [אַבְרָהָם] — for a father of a multitude of nations [כִּי אַב-הֲמוֹן גּוֹיִם] I have made you [נְתַתִּיךָ].

**17:6** And I will make you fruitful [וְהִפְרֵתִי אֹתְךָ] exceedingly [בִּמְאֹד מְאֹד] and I will make you [וּנְתַתִּיךָ] into nations [לְגוֹיִם] and kings [וּמְלָכִים] will go out from you [מִמְּךָ יֵצֵאוּ].

**17:7** And I will establish [וַהֲקִמֹתִי] my covenant [אֶת-בְּרִיתִי] between me and between you [בֵּינִי וּבֵינֶךָ] and between your seed after you [וּבֵין זַרְעֲךָ אַחֲרֶיךָ] to their generations [לְדֹרֹתָם] for a covenant of eternity [לִבְרִית עוֹלָם] — to be God to you [לִהְיוֹת לְךָ לֵאלֹהִים] and to your seed after you [וּלְזַרְעֲךָ אַחֲרֶיךָ].

**17:8** And I will give [וְנָתַתִּי] to you and to your seed after you [לְךָ וּלְזַרְעֲךָ אַחֲרֶיךָ] the land of your sojournings [אֵת אֶרֶץ מְגֻרֶיךָ] — all the land of Canaan [אֵת כָּל-אֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן] — for an everlasting possession [לַאֲחֻזַּת עוֹלָם] and I will be [וְהָיִיתִי] their God [לָהֶם לֵאלֹהִים].

---

*The Covenant of Circumcision — Sign of the Covenant (vv. 9–14)*

**17:9** And God said [וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים] to Abraham [אֶל-אַבְרָהָם]: And as for you [וְאַתָּה] — you shall keep [תִּשְׁמֹר] my covenant [אֶת-בְּרִיתִי] — you [אַתָּה] and your seed after you [וְזַרְעֲךָ אַחֲרֶיךָ] to their generations [לְדֹרֹתָם].

**17:10** This [זֹאת] is my covenant [בְּרִיתִי] which you shall keep [אֲשֶׁר תִּשְׁמְרוּ] between me and between you [בֵּינִי וּבֵינֵיכֶם] and between your seed after you [וּבֵין זַרְעֲךָ אַחֲרֶיךָ] — let every male among you be circumcised [הִמּוֹל לָכֶם כָּל-זָכָר].

**17:11** And you shall circumcise [וּנְמַלְתֶּם] the flesh of your foreskin [אֵת בְּשַׂר עָרְלַתְכֶם] and it will be [וְהָיָה] for a sign of covenant [לְאוֹת בְּרִית] between me and between you [בֵּינִי וּבֵינֵיכֶם].

**17:12** And the son of eight days [וּבֶן-שְׁמֹנַת יָמִים] — let him be circumcised among you [יִמּוֹל לָכֶם] — every male [כָּל-זָכָר] to your generations [לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶם] — the house-born [יְלִיד בָּיִת] and the purchased of silver [וּמִקְנַת-כֶּסֶף] from any foreigner [מִכֹּל בֶּן-נֵכָר] who is not [אֲשֶׁר לֹא] of your seed [מִזַּרְעֲךָ הוּא].

**17:13** Circumcising shall be circumcised [הִמּוֹל יִמּוֹל] the house-born [יְלִיד בֵּיתְךָ] and the purchased of your silver [וּמִקְנַת כַּסְפֶּךָ] and my covenant will be [וְהָיְתָה בְרִיתִי] in your flesh [בִּבְשַׂרְכֶם] for a covenant of eternity [לִבְרִית עוֹלָם].

**17:14** And the uncircumcised male [וְעָרֵל זָכָר] whose flesh of his foreskin [אֲשֶׁר לֹא-יִמּוֹל אֶת-בְּשַׂר עָרְלָתוֹ] is not circumcised — that soul [וְנִכְרְתָה הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַהִוא] will be cut off [מֵעַמֶּיהָ] from its people — my covenant [אֶת-בְּרִיתִי] he has broken [הֵפַר].

---

*Sarai's Name Changed to Sarah — She Will Bear a Son (vv. 15–16)*

**17:15** And God said [וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים] to Abraham [אֶל-אַבְרָהָם]: Sarai your wife [שָׂרַי אִשְׁתְּךָ] — you shall not call [לֹא-תִקְרָא] her name [אֶת-שְׁמָהּ] Sarai [שָׂרָי] — for Sarah [כִּי שָׂרָה] is her name [שְׁמָהּ].

**17:16** And I will bless her [וּבֵרַכְתִּי אֹתָהּ] and I will also give you [וְגַם נָתַתִּי מִמֶּנָּה לְךָ] from her a son [בֵּן] and I will bless her [וּבֵרַכְתִּיהָ] and she will be [וְהָיְתָה] for nations [לְגוֹיִם] — kings of peoples [מַלְכֵי עַמִּים] will come [יִהְיוּ] from her [מִמֶּנָּה].

---

*Abraham Laughs — He Pleads for Ishmael (vv. 17–18)*

**17:17** And Abraham fell [וַיִּפֹּל אַבְרָהָם] on his face [עַל-פָּנָיו] and laughed [וַיִּצְחָק] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר] in his heart [בְּלִבּוֹ]: To the son of a hundred years [הַלְבֶן מֵאָה-שָׁנָה] will a child be born [יִוָּלֵד] and if Sarah [וְאִם-שָׂרָה] the daughter of ninety years [הֲבַת-תִּשְׁעִים שָׁנָה] will bear [תֵּלֵד]?

**17:18** And Abraham said [וַיֹּאמֶר אַבְרָהָם] to God [אֶל-הָאֱלֹהִים]: If only Ishmael [לוּ יִשְׁמָעֵאל] might live [יִחְיֶה] before you [לְפָנֶיךָ]!

---

*God Affirms Isaac — And Blesses Ishmael (vv. 19–22)*

**17:19** And God said [וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים]: Indeed [אֲבָל] — Sarah your wife [שָׂרָה אִשְׁתְּךָ] will bear you [יֹלֶדֶת לְךָ] a son [בֵּן] and you shall call his name [וְקָרָאתָ אֶת-שְׁמוֹ] Isaac [יִצְחָק] and I will establish [וַהֲקִמֹתִי] my covenant [אֶת-בְּרִיתִי] with him [אִתּוֹ] for a covenant of eternity [לִבְרִית עוֹלָם] and with his seed after him [לְזַרְעוֹ אַחֲרָיו].

**17:20** And as for Ishmael [וּלְיִשְׁמָעֵאל] — I have heard you [שְׁמַעְתִּיךָ] — look [הִנֵּה] I have blessed him [בֵּרַכְתִּי אֹתוֹ] and I will make him fruitful [וְהִפְרֵיתִי אֹתוֹ] and I will multiply him [וְהִרְבֵּיתִי אֹתוֹ] exceedingly [בִּמְאֹד מְאֹד] — twelve princes [שְׁנֵים-עָשָׂר נְשִׂיאִם] he will beget [יוֹלִיד] and I will make him [וּנְתַתִּיו] into a great nation [לְגוֹי גָּדוֹל].

**17:21** And my covenant [וְאֶת-בְּרִיתִי] I will establish [אָקִים] with Isaac [אֶת-יִצְחָק] whom Sarah will bear to you [אֲשֶׁר תֵּלֵד לְךָ שָׂרָה] at this appointed time [לַמּוֹעֵד הַזֶּה] in the next year [בַּשָּׁנָה הָאַחֶרֶת].

**17:22** And he finished [וַיְכַל] speaking [לְדַבֵּר] with him [אִתּוֹ] and God went up [וַיַּעַל אֱלֹהִים] from upon Abraham [מֵעַל אַבְרָהָם].

---

*Abraham Circumcises His Household (vv. 23–27)*

**17:23** And Abraham took [וַיִּקַּח אַבְרָהָם] Ishmael his son [אֶת-יִשְׁמָעֵאל בְּנוֹ] and all the house-born of his house [וְאֵת כָּל-יְלִידֵי בֵיתוֹ] and all the purchased of his silver [וְאֵת כָּל-מִקְנַת כַּסְפּוֹ] — every male [כָּל-זָכָר] among the men of Abraham's house [בְּאַנְשֵׁי בֵּית אַבְרָהָם] — and he circumcised [וַיָּמָל] the flesh of their foreskin [אֶת-בְּשַׂר עָרְלָתָם] in the bone of that very day [בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה] just as [כַּאֲשֶׁר] God had spoken [דִּבֶּר] with him [אִתּוֹ].

**17:24** And Abraham [וְאַבְרָהָם] was the son of ninety-nine years [בֶּן-תִּשְׁעִים וָתֵשַׁע שָׁנָה] when he was circumcised [בְּהִמֹּלוֹ] in the flesh of his foreskin [בְּשַׂר עָרְלָתוֹ].

**17:25** And Ishmael his son [וְיִשְׁמָעֵאל בְּנוֹ] was the son of thirteen years [בֶּן-שְׁלֹשׁ עֶשְׂרֵה שָׁנָה] when he was circumcised [בְּהִמֹּלוֹ] in the flesh of his foreskin [בְּשַׂר עָרְלָתוֹ].

**17:26** In the bone of that very day [בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה] were circumcised [נִמּוֹל] Abraham [אַבְרָהָם] and Ishmael his son [וְיִשְׁמָעֵאל בְּנוֹ].

**17:27** And all the men of his house [וְכָל-אַנְשֵׁי בֵיתוֹ] — the house-born [יְלִיד בָּיִת] and the purchased of silver [וּמִקְנַת-כֶּסֶף] from a foreigner [מֵאֵת בֶּן-נֵכָר] — were circumcised with him [נִמּוֹלוּ אִתּוֹ].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### On 17:1 — אֲנִי-אֵל שַׁדַּי: El Shaddai (Key Crux)

**אֲנִי-אֵל שַׁדַּי** — "I am El Shaddai."

This divine self-disclosure introduces the name **שַׁדַּי** (Shaddai) for the first time in Genesis as part of a compound divine name. The name **אֵל שַׁדַּי** (El Shaddai) appears six times in Genesis (17:1; 28:3; 35:11; 43:14; 48:3; 49:25) and becomes the characteristic patriarchal name for God — Exodus 6:3 makes this explicit: "I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob as El Shaddai, but by my name YHWH I was not known to them." The name Shaddai alone (without El) appears 31 times in the Hebrew Bible, concentrated heavily in Job (31 occurrences).

**The etymology of שַׁדַּי (Shaddai) is genuinely uncertain** — one of the most debated problems in Hebrew onomastics:

1. **"Almighty" (traditional):** The LXX renders El Shaddai as **παντοκράτωρ** (pantokrator, "Almighty, ruler of all") and the Vulgate as *omnipotens* — this becomes the standard English "God Almighty." But this rendering is interpretive rather than etymological; it reflects the *function* of the God so named rather than a derivation of the word itself.

2. **"Mountain" (שַׁד = shadh):** Some scholars connect Shaddai to an Akkadian cognate *shadu* ("mountain") — El of the Mountain, the Mountain God. This accords with ancient Near Eastern divine epithets (Baal of the heights, the God of the mountain) and fits the patriarchal context of mountain-theophany. The form would be **שַׁדַּי** as a relational adjective: "the Mountain One."

3. **"Breast" (שַׁד = shadh):** The word **שַׁד** in Hebrew means "breast" — so **שַׁדַּי** could mean "the Breasted One" or "the Nurturer." This reading connects El Shaddai specifically to fertility and blessing — which precisely fits its usage in Genesis: every time El Shaddai appears it accompanies a blessing of fruitfulness and multiplication (17:1-2: covenant of fruitfulness; 28:3: "El Shaddai bless you and make you fruitful and multiply you"; 35:11: "I am El Shaddai — be fruitful and multiply"; 49:25: "from El Shaddai who will bless you — blessings of the breasts [שָׁדַיִם] and womb"). The association of Shaddai with **שָׁדַיִם** (shadayim, "breasts") in 49:25 is either a deliberate wordplay or a genuine etymological connection.

4. **"Destroyer" (שָׁדַד = shadad):** Some connect Shaddai to the root **שָׁדַד** ("to destroy, devastate, deal violently") — the Destructive One, the Overwhelming One. This gives the name a more terrible character — God as the one who overwhelms and overpowers.

5. **"He of the field/wilderness" (Aramaic shadu):** Some propose a connection to Aramaic or Arabic roots meaning "field, open country" — the God of the wild places.

**None of these etymologies is conclusive.** The EMET retains the untranslated form "El Shaddai" as the most honest rendering, rather than imposing any single English equivalent. The name is a proper name given to God by the ancient tradition; its connotations of power, fertility, and overwhelming divine presence are all present in the contexts of its use, regardless of which etymology is correct.

**הִתְהַלֵּךְ לְפָנַי וֶהְיֵה תָמִים** — "walk before me and be whole."

The verb **הִתְהַלֵּךְ** (Hitpael of **הָלַךְ**, "to walk") is the reflexive-intensive: "walk yourself continuously" — a walk that is an ongoing, active, self-directing journey. **לְפָנַי** — "before my face / before me" — implies a life lived in the awareness of divine presence, oriented toward God. The same phrase is used of Enoch (5:22, 24: "Enoch walked with God") and of Noah (6:9: "a righteous man, whole — with God Noah walked"). Now Abraham receives the same call.

**תָמִים** (tamim) — "whole, complete, blameless, without defect." The word is used for an animal without physical blemish acceptable for sacrifice (Lev. 1:3, 10), for the man of integrity (Ps. 15:2), and for God's own ways (Deut. 32:4: "the Rock — his work is whole [תָמִים]"). It does not mean moral perfection in an absolute sense but *wholeness* — integrity, undividedness, a life in which the inner self matches the outer, in which commitment is not partial or hedged. The call to Abraham at 99 years of age, thirteen years after Ishmael's birth and just before the covenant of circumcision, is a call to complete alignment: walk before El Shaddai with your whole self.

---

### On 17:5 — שִׁמְךָ אַבְרָהָם: The Name Change (Key Crux)

**וְלֹא-יִקָּרֵא שִׁמְךָ עוֹד אַבְרָם וְהָיָה שִׁמְךָ אַבְרָהָם** — "And your name will no longer be called Abram, and your name will be Abraham."

This is the first of two name changes in Genesis 17 (the other: Sarai to Sarah in v. 15). The change from **אַבְרָם** (Avram) to **אַבְרָהָם** (Avraham) is of enormous theological weight, but its precise linguistics require honest evaluation.

**The traditional etymology offered by the text itself (v. 5b):** "for a father of a multitude of nations I have made you" — **כִּי אַב-הֲמוֹן גּוֹיִם נְתַתִּיךָ**. The text does not parse the name etymologically; it provides a theological explanation: Abraham is the name of one who will be a father (**אָב**, av) of a multitude (**הֲמוֹן**, hamon) of nations.

**The linguistic reality:** The name **אַבְרָהָם** is most likely a dialectal or phonological variant of **אַבְרָם**, not a true transformation with a new morphological element. Both names appear to mean approximately the same thing:
- **אַבְרָם** = **אָב** ("father") + **רָם** ("exalted") = "the father is exalted" or "exalted father."
- **אַבְרָהָם** = likely the same root expanded, possibly **אָב** + **רַהַם** (an archaic form) or simply an extended form. The **-ה-** inserted into **Avram** to make **Avraham** is not a standard Hebrew morphological process that adds the word **הֲמוֹן** ("multitude") — the sound-similarity to **אב-המון** is a theological wordplay, not a strict etymology.

**This is a theological name-change, not a linguistic etymology.** The narrative is not claiming that Abraham's name literally means "father of multitudes" as a morphological derivation; it is asserting that the man whose name will henceforth be Abraham is by divine declaration the ancestor of a multitude. The new name (or expanded form of the old name) becomes the carrier of the promise: every time the name is spoken, it echoes the covenant.

The LXX does not transliterate the name differently but renders the verse with **ἔσται τὸ ὄνομά σου Αβρααμ** — "your name will be Abraham" — and explains it as "I have made you a father of many nations" (πατέρα πολλῶν ἐθνῶν τέθεικά σε), following the MT's own explanation.

The EMET preserves the English name form "Abraham" (following convention) and notes the theological wordplay without imposing it as an etymology.

---

### On 17:7 — בְּרִית עוֹלָם: Covenant of Eternity

**לִבְרִית עוֹלָם** — "for a covenant of eternity."

The phrase **בְּרִית עוֹלָם** (berit olam, "everlasting/eternal covenant") is one of the most theologically charged formulas in the Pentateuch. The noun **עוֹלָם** (olam) means "eternity, perpetuity, the furthest extent of time" — it looks both backward (antiquity, the primordial) and forward (perpetuity, the indefinite future). A **בְּרִית עוֹלָם** is not a covenant with an expiration date; it is a commitment that defines the entire span of time.

The phrase occurs in Genesis 17 at three points: v. 7 (the covenant with Abraham and his seed), v. 13 (circumcision as the sign of the everlasting covenant in the flesh), and v. 19 (the covenant established with Isaac). Its repetition three times within this chapter underscores that circumcision is not a provisional or temporary ordinance but a mark of a permanent divine commitment. The same phrase is used for God's covenant with Noah (9:16: "the everlasting covenant between God and every living creature"), the Sabbath (Exod. 31:16), the covenant with Phinehas (Num. 25:13), and the covenant with David (2 Sam. 23:5).

---

### On 17:8 — אֶרֶץ מְגֻרֶיךָ: Land of Your Sojournings

**אֵת אֶרֶץ מְגֻרֶיךָ** — "the land of your sojournings."

The word **מְגוּרִים** (megurim, pl. of **מָגוֹר**) derives from the root **גּוּר** (gur, "to sojourn, to live as a resident alien, to dwell temporarily"). Abraham has been a **גֵּר** — a sojourner — in the land of Canaan (12:10; 15:13: "your seed will be sojourners [גֵּר] in a land not theirs"). The land is called "the land of your sojournings" precisely because Abraham has never possessed it; he has wandered through it as a stranger. God now promises to give this very land — the land where Abraham is a foreigner — as an eternal possession. The irony is deliberate: the sojourner will become the ancestor of the possessors. The word **מְגֻרֶיךָ** (megurekha) echoes **גֵּר** and points forward to Israel's experience as sojourners in Egypt (Exod. 6:4 explicitly connects this verse to the Egyptian sojourn).

---

### On 17:11 — אוֹת בְּרִית: Sign of the Covenant

**וְהָיָה לְאוֹת בְּרִית בֵּינִי וּבֵינֵיכֶם** — "and it will be for a sign of covenant between me and between you."

**אוֹת** (ot, "sign, token, mark") is the word used for the rainbow as the sign of the Noahic covenant (9:12-13, 17: "this is the **אוֹת** of the covenant"). The structural parallel is exact and programmatic: just as the rainbow is the visible sign embedded in creation that marks the covenant with Noah, so circumcision is the physical sign embedded in the body that marks the covenant with Abraham. The theological architecture of Genesis is built on these covenant-signs: visible markers that carry the weight of an invisible divine commitment.

The difference is significant: the rainbow is a natural phenomenon — already present in creation, reinterpreted as covenant-sign. Circumcision is a human act — a cutting of the body, a physical alteration that marks the covenant-bearer permanently. The Noahic sign is seen in the sky; the Abrahamic sign is carried in the flesh. The sign is not merely external but inscribed on the body of every covenant male, making the covenant physically present wherever the covenant community goes.

---

### On 17:14 — וְנִכְרְתָה הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַהִוא: Karet — Cut Off (Key Crux — The Circumcision Irony)

**וְנִכְרְתָה הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַהִוא מֵעַמֶּיהָ אֶת-בְּרִיתִי הֵפַר** — "that soul will be cut off from its people — my covenant he has broken."

This verse contains one of the most striking ironies in the entire Pentateuch — what the EMET calls the **karet/circumcision irony**.

**The verb in question:** The covenant is described throughout Genesis as being **כָּרַת** (karat) — literally "cut." The standard phrase for covenant-making is **כָּרַת בְּרִית** — "to cut a covenant" (15:18: "on that day YHWH **cut** a covenant with Abram"). The covenant is established by cutting — whether the cutting of animals in 15:10 (the covenant ceremony between the pieces) or the act of circumcision (cutting the foreskin) in 17:11-13.

Now in v. 14, the word for the punishment of the uncircumcised man is **נִכְרְתָה** — the Niphal of **כָּרַת** — "he will be **cut off**." The same root that names the covenant-making act (cutting a covenant) and the covenant sign (cutting the flesh) is now used for the punishment of the one who refuses the covenant sign: he will be **cut** — excommunicated, excluded, severed from the people.

**The irony is complete and intentional:**
- The covenant is made by **cutting** (כָּרַת בְּרִית).
- The covenant sign is **cutting** (circumcision — **מוּל**, "to cut/circumcise").
- The one who refuses to be **cut** in the covenant sign will himself be **cut off** (כָּרַת) from the covenant people.

The one who will not bear the cut of covenant will experience the cut of exclusion. The instrument of inclusion (cutting = covenant sign) becomes, by its absence, the instrument of exclusion (cutting off = karet punishment). The Hebrew pun is untranslatable but unmistakable.

**כָּרֵת (karet):** The penalty "cut off from the people" (כָּרֵת) is a distinct category of divine punishment in the Pentateuch — approximately 36 offenses carry this penalty in Leviticus and Numbers. It is not the death penalty (which requires human execution) but a divine removal — whether by premature death, childlessness, or exclusion from the community — the precise mechanism is debated in rabbinic literature. But the severity is clear: the uncircumcised man has broken God's covenant (v. 14b: **בְּרִיתִי הֵפַר** — "my covenant he has broken") and receives the severest non-capital consequence the Torah knows.

**הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַהִוא** — "that soul." **נֶּפֶשׁ** (nefesh) here is used for the individual person — "that person, that soul" — the complete living human being, not merely an inner spiritual component. The karet falls on the whole person.

---

### On 17:15 — שָׂרַי → שָׂרָה: Sarai to Sarah

**שָׂרַי אִשְׁתְּךָ לֹא-תִקְרָא אֶת-שְׁמָהּ שָׂרָי כִּי שָׂרָה שְׁמָהּ** — "Sarai your wife — you shall not call her name Sarai — for Sarah is her name."

The name change from **שָׂרַי** (Saray) to **שָׂרָה** (Sarah) is linguistically simpler than Abram to Abraham: both forms derive from the same root **שָׂרָה** / **שֹׂר** meaning "princess, noblewoman, lady of rank." The difference may be dialectal — **שָׂרַי** with a yod-suffix (a first-person possessive suffix in some archaic or dialectal forms: "my princess") and **שָׂרָה** as the more standard form without it. If **שָׂרַי** carries the possessive, then Sarai means "my princess" — possibly a personal or pet name — and Sarah means simply "princess" or "noblewoman" — a name of broader and more public scope. By becoming "Sarah" she ceases to be anyone's particular princess and becomes "the princess" — the matriarch.

Unlike the Abram-Abraham change, the text offers no etymological explanation for the Sarai-Sarah change — no "for she will be..." equivalent. The name change speaks for itself: Sarah will be the mother of kings (v. 16: "kings of peoples will come from her").

The LXX renders both names as **Σαρα** (Sara) — not distinguishing between them graphically. This is likely because the difference in voweling (**שָׂרַי** vs **שָׂרָה**) between the Hebrew forms does not produce a clear Greek distinction. The narrative point (a new name = a new identity = a new covenant role) is nonetheless present.

---

### On 17:17 — וַיִּצְחָק: Abraham Laughs — Isaac Named (Key Crux)

**וַיִּפֹּל אַבְרָהָם עַל-פָּנָיו וַיִּצְחָק** — "And Abraham fell on his face and laughed."

**וַיִּצְחָק** (wayyitschaq) — "and he laughed." This is the Qal wayyiqtol 3ms of **צָחַק** (tsachaq, "to laugh"). The form **יִצְחַק** (yitschaq) is identical to the name **יִצְחָק** — Isaac (Yitschaq). The verb is indistinguishable from the name: "Abraham fell on his face and **Yitschaq'd**" — he "Isaac'd." The wordplay is built into the Hebrew text: the name of the child to be born is present as the verb used to describe Abraham's reaction to the announcement of that birth.

This is the **yitschaq/laughter** crux of the chapter. The name Isaac (**יִצְחָק**) will be explained three times in Genesis:
- **17:17** — Abraham laughs (**וַיִּצְחָק**) at the announcement (laughter of astonishment or disbelief)
- **17:19** — God commands: "you shall call his name Isaac" — the name carries the laughter
- **18:12** — Sarah laughs within herself at the announcement (laughter of incredulity)
- **21:6** — Sarah says at Isaac's birth: "God has made laughter for me" (**צְחֹק עָשָׂה לִי אֱלֹהִים**) — "everyone who hears will laugh for me"

The name **יִצְחָק** is a Qal (or Hiphil) imperfect 3ms — "he laughs" or "he will laugh" or "may he laugh." It is a sentence-name: "[God/El] laughs" or simply "laughter." The child is named Laughter — carrying within his name the astonishment of his parents and the joy of his miraculous birth.

**The double content of Abraham's laughter:** Abraham's laughter here (v. 17) is **within himself** — the narrative does not say he laughed openly before God but that he fell on his face (reverence/prostration) and laughed **in his heart** (**בְּלִבּוֹ**). The inner laughter is the human response to an announcement that has crossed from the merely astonishing to the biologically incredible: a 100-year-old man and a 90-year-old woman. Abraham is not mocking God — he falls on his face in reverence — but the private laughter of an old man's heart at the thought of what is being promised is real and is not rebuked. God does not rebuke Abraham for laughing; God rebukes Sarah's laughter in 18:13-15 (though even there the rebuke is mild). The difference may be: Abraham's laughter is private and accompanied by prostration; Sarah's is also private but is followed by denial when caught.

**The plea for Ishmael (v. 18):** Coming immediately after the laughter, Abraham's prayer "if only Ishmael might live before you!" reveals what is in the laughter: it is not simply mockery of a biological impossibility but a father's love for his existing son. Abraham already has a son — Ishmael, whom he loves. The promise of another son feels simultaneously wonderful and unnecessary. "Let Ishmael be enough" is the heart of his prayer. God's response (vv. 19-21) is gracious to both: Ishmael will be blessed with multiplication and twelve princes, but the covenant line runs through the son who is yet to be born — Isaac.

---

### On 17:19 — יִצְחָק: Isaac Named by God

**וְקָרָאתָ אֶת-שְׁמוֹ יִצְחָק** — "and you shall call his name Isaac."

God names the child before his conception, as he named Ishmael (16:11) before his birth. The divine ante-natal naming confirms Isaac's identity as a covenant child: his entire existence is constituted by the divine word before he exists. The name **יִצְחָק** — Laughter — carries forward the laughter of 17:17 and anticipates the laughter of 18:12-15 and 21:6. The child of promise arrives trailing laughter behind him, the laughter of the impossible made actual.

**וַהֲקִמֹתִי אֶת-בְּרִיתִי אִתּוֹ לִבְרִית עוֹלָם** — "and I will establish my covenant with him for a covenant of eternity."

The covenant formula reaches its apex: **my covenant** (covenant possession: **אֶת-בְּרִיתִי**) — **I will establish** (**הֲקִים**, the verb of establishing, raising up, confirming) — **with him** — **for a covenant of eternity** (**לִבְרִית עוֹלָם**). This is the third occurrence of **בְּרִית עוֹלָם** in the chapter (vv. 7, 13, 19), anchoring the covenant across the narrative: it is everlasting, it passes through Isaac, it is established not improvised.

---

### On 17:20 — יִשְׁמָעֵאל: God Hears Abraham's Plea

**שְׁמַעְתִּיךָ** — "I have heard you."

The verb **שָׁמַע** (shama', "to hear") appears here in a pointed form: God says to Abraham "I have heard you" (1cs perfect with 2ms suffix) regarding Ishmael. The name **יִשְׁמָעֵאל** means "God hears" — and now God confirms that he has heard Abraham's prayer for Ishmael. The name of the son is the divine action: hearing. When Abraham prays for Ishmael, God says "I have heard you" — the very thing Ishmael's name declares. The name was given because God heard Hagar's affliction (16:11); now the name is confirmed because God heard Abraham's love for his son.

**שְׁנֵים-עָשָׂר נְשִׂיאִם** — "twelve princes."

The twelve princes of Ishmael (listed in 25:13-16) are: Nebaioth, Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam, Mishma, Dumah, Massa, Hadad, Tema, Jetur, Naphish, Kedemah. Twelve — structurally parallel to the twelve sons of Jacob/Israel (and thus the twelve tribes). The parallelism is not accidental: God's providential ordering of history through both lineages produces a twelvefold structure in each. Ishmael's twelve princes correspond to Israel's twelve tribes — twin trees from one root, Abraham.

---

### On 17:21 — לַמּוֹעֵד הַזֶּה בַּשָּׁנָה הָאַחֶרֶת: The Appointed Time

**לַמּוֹעֵד הַזֶּה בַּשָּׁנָה הָאַחֶרֶת** — "at this appointed time in the next year."

**מוֹעֵד** (mo'ed, "appointed time, fixed time, set meeting") is the word used for the sacred times and festivals of Israel (Lev. 23:2: "these are the **מוֹעֲדֵי** of the LORD"). It carries the sense of a divinely fixed appointment — not merely "next year at this time" as casual scheduling but "at this appointed season" — a time God has fixed for the fulfillment of the promise. The birth of Isaac is an appointment God has made with creation. The LXX renders **κατὰ τὸν καιρὸν τοῦτον εἰς ὥρας** — "at this time, at the [appointed] hour" — using **καιρός** (kairos, the moment of opportunity/appointment) for **מוֹעֵד**. The word will recur in 18:14 (the three men confirm: "at the appointed time [לַמּוֹעֵד] I will return to you") — a double announcement of the same divine appointment.

---

### On 17:23 — בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה: In the Bone of That Very Day

**בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה** — "in the bone of that very day."

The idiom **בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם** (be'etsem hayyom) — literally "in the bone/substance/essence of the day" — is a Hebrew superlative of temporal emphasis: "on that very day itself, in the full daylight of that day, immediately and without delay." **עֶצֶם** (etsem) means "bone, substance, self" — used to intensify: the essential, undeniable reality of the day. The phrase appears at critical moments of covenant or divine action (also in v. 26; Exod. 12:17, 41, 51; Lev. 23:14, 21, 28-30; Deut. 32:48 — the very day Moses climbs Nebo to die). In every case it signals that the divine command is executed without hesitation, without delay — the obedience is immediate and total.

Abraham's response to the covenant of circumcision is paradigmatic obedience: on the very day God speaks, Abraham circumcises his entire household. There is no delay of consultation, no period of adjustment, no gradual compliance. The covenant is received and the sign is implemented **in the bone of that very day**. This immediacy is held up as the definition of faithful covenant response.

The repetition of the phrase in vv. 23 and 26 brackets the account of the circumcision: Abraham acts on the very day (v. 23), and the event is confirmed as having occurred on that very day (v. 26). The narrative emphasis is on the completeness and immediacy of obedience.

---

## LXX Comparison Summary for Genesis 17

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Codex Vaticanus) | Notes |
|-------|---------------------|----------------------|-------|
| 17:1 | אֲנִי-אֵל שַׁדַּי | ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ θεός σου | LXX: "I am your God" — does not render Shaddai as a distinct name but substitutes the generic "your God." Elsewhere LXX renders Shaddai as παντοκράτωρ ("Almighty") |
| 17:1 | תָמִים | ἄμεμπτος | LXX: "blameless" (ἄμεμπτος = without blame, faultless). MT **תָמִים** = "whole/complete." LXX interprets wholeness as moral blamelessness |
| 17:4 | לְאַב הֲמוֹן גּוֹיִם | πατέρα πλήθους ἐθνῶν | LXX: "father of a multitude of nations" — exact rendering. πλῆθος = multitude |
| 17:5 | אַבְרָהָם | Αβρααμ | LXX transliterates; explains as πατέρα πολλῶν ἐθνῶν τέθεικά σε ("I have made you a father of many nations") |
| 17:7 | לִבְרִית עוֹלָם | εἰς διαθήκην αἰώνιον | LXX: "for an eternal covenant" — διαθήκη (diatheke) = covenant/testament; αἰώνιος = eternal. Standard LXX rendering |
| 17:11 | לְאוֹת בְּרִית | εἰς σημεῖον διαθήκης | LXX: "for a sign of covenant" — exact. σημεῖον = sign |
| 17:14 | וְנִכְרְתָה הַנֶּפֶשׁ | ἐξολεθρευθήσεται ἡ ψυχή | LXX: "that soul will be utterly destroyed" — ἐξολεθρεύω (to destroy completely) is stronger than MT's "cut off" (כָּרַת). The karet irony (cut/cut off) is not reproducible in Greek |
| 17:15 | שָׂרַי / שָׂרָה | Σαρα (both times) | LXX renders both Sarai and Sarah as Σαρα — the Hebrew distinction is lost in Greek transliteration |
| 17:17 | וַיִּצְחָק | κατεγέλασεν | LXX: "he laughed" — κατεγελάω (to laugh at, laugh down). The LXX uses a verb with a slightly derisive tone. The Isaac-wordplay (**יִצְחָק** = "Isaac" = "he laughs") is entirely lost in Greek |
| 17:19 | יִצְחָק | Ισαακ | LXX transliterates the name. The naming explanation ("you shall call his name Isaac") gives no etymology in either MT or LXX — the laughter connection is borne by context, not etymology in v. 19 |
| 17:21 | לַמּוֹעֵד הַזֶּה | κατὰ τὸν καιρὸν τοῦτον | LXX: "at this time/season" — καιρός (appointed time/opportunity) for **מוֹעֵד** (appointed time). Exact semantic equivalent |
| 17:23 | בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה | ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ ἐκείνῃ | LXX: "on that day" — loses the intensifying "in the bone/very" aspect of **בְּעֶצֶם**. The immediacy-emphasis is flattened |

---

## Dead Sea Scrolls and Versional Notes

**Dead Sea Scrolls:** No significant Genesis 17 fragments are extant from Qumran. As noted in the manifest, the chapter falls in a gap in the preserved scrolls. The Samaritan Pentateuch agrees closely with the Leningrad Codex for Genesis 17 with minor orthographic variants only.

**Targum Onkelos on 17:1:** Renders **אֵל שַׁדַּי** as **אֵל שַׁדַּי** — preserving the divine name untranslated in Aramaic, not interpreting it. The Targum on 17:1 adds the phrase "whose word is sufficient" (a midrashic gloss reading Shaddai as connected to **שֶׁ-דַּי** = "who/that-is-enough" = "the Self-Sufficient One"). This reading, though popular in later tradition (midrash and Rashi), is a folk etymology rather than a scholarly derivation but reflects the ancient Jewish instinct that Shaddai designates divine self-sufficiency.

**Targum Onkelos on 17:14:** Renders the karet penalty as "that soul will be uprooted from her people" — **תֶּעֱקַר נַפְשָׁא הַהִיא** — using the Aramaic verb for "uprooting." The metaphor of uprooting (removing from the soil, severing connection) is the Targum's rendering of "cut off" and preserves the force of covenant-severance without the verbal irony.

**Josephus (Antiquities I.10.5):** Josephus reports the circumcision of Abraham's household and notes that Abraham was circumcised at 99 years of age and Ishmael at 13. He records the Ishmael-blessing briefly. He does not engage the El Shaddai question. He renders the Isaac-laughter episode sympathetically, presenting Abraham's astonishment as natural given the ages involved.

**Philo (De Abrahamo, De Migratione Abrahami):** Philo allegorizes the name change from Abram to Abraham extensively: "Abram" means "uplifted father" (a soul that has not yet reached perfection, seeking wisdom above but not yet possessing it); "Abraham" means "elect father of sound" (the soul that has received wisdom and become a teacher of others). This Platonic-allegorical reading is historically influential but moves far from the literal-grammatical sense. The EMET notes it as a trajectory without endorsing it as the primary meaning.

**On the Covenant of Circumcision and the Rainbow Covenant (structural parallel):** The narrative architecture of Genesis places circumcision in deliberate structural parallel with the rainbow:
- Genesis 9:12-17: **Rainbow** — sign (**אוֹת**) of the Noahic covenant (**בְּרִית עוֹלָם**), embedded in creation, seen in the sky after rain.
- Genesis 17:11-13: **Circumcision** — sign (**אוֹת**) of the Abrahamic covenant (**בְּרִית עוֹלָם**), embedded in the body, carried in the flesh of every covenant male.
Both signs are called **אוֹת** (sign) and **בְּרִית עוֹלָם** (everlasting covenant). Both are given as guarantees of a divine promise that transcends human merit. The Noahic sign guarantees the continuity of creation; the Abrahamic sign marks the people through whom the purposes of creation will be fulfilled.
